Table 8 Quantitative comparison of different high-frequency operators for the structural prior. Best results are highlighted in bold.

Fig. 1. Overall architecture of the proposed ZID-Net. The network adopts a dual-stream deterministic backbone regularized by a diffusion-based prior. (a) The Semantic Context Block (SCB) extracts multi-scale context features from the input hazy image. (b) The LGCBs aggregate scene-level deep semantic features at the bottleneck stage. (c) The CSLM extracts and adaptively filters high-frequency physical residuals using a Laplacian pyramid. (d) The Color-Aware Structural Block (CASB) extracts high-frequency structural features. (e) The ZI-PPH regularizes the bottleneck

Table 7 Quantitative comparison of different auxiliary supervision heads during training on the Real-World Thin dataset. All auxiliary heads are detached during inference. Best results are highlighted in bold.

Fig. 3. Detailed architecture of the ZI-PPH branch. This training-only auxiliary module regularizes the shared bottleneck features ?̂?𝑏 via a conditional U-Net. The noisy physical residual 𝑅𝑡 and ?̂?𝑏 are concatenated at the input level, with the timestep 𝑡 (encoded via SPE and MLP) additively injected across the network. This branch predicts the added noise 𝜖̂ to embed haze-severity priors into the shared representation.
